elements.sent-2411-005: Rizzieri\inst{\ref{Oxford \institute{ International School for Advanced Studies (SISSA), Via Bonomea 265, 34136, Trieste, Italy \and Istituto Nazionale di Fisica Nucleare (INFN), Sezione di Trieste, via Valerio 2, 34127 Trieste, Italy \and Institut de Recherche en Astrophysique et Planétologie (IRAP), Universit\'e de Toulouse, CNRS, CNES, UPS, 9 Av.}}}
elements.sent-2411-006: The variation of the physical conditions across the three dimensions of our Galaxy is a major source of complexity for the modelling of the foreground signal facing the cosmic microwave background (CMB).
elements.sent-2411-007: In the present work, we demonstrate that the spin-moment expansion formalism provides a powerful framework to model and understand this complexity, with a special focus on that arising from variations of the physical conditions along each line-of-sight on the sky.
elements.sent-2411-008: We perform the first application of the moment expansion to reproduce a thermal dust model largely used by the CMB community, demonstrating its power as a minimal tool to compress, understand and model the information contained within any foreground model.
elements.sent-2411-009: Furthermore, we use this framework to produce new models of thermal dust emission containing the maximal amount of complexity allowed by the current data, remaining compatible with the observed angular power-spectra by the \planck{ mission.}
elements.sent-2411-010: By assessing the impact of these models on the performance of component separation methodologies, we conclude that the additional complexity contained within the third dimension could represent a significant challenge for future CMB experiments and that different component separation approaches are sensitive to different properties of the moments.
elements.sent-2411-011: Our modern understanding of cosmology heavily relies on the precise characterisation of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) signal.
elements.sent-2411-012: The temperature anisotropies of the CMB have been accurately mapped over the whole sky, validating the canonical $\Lambda$-CDM model as the best-fit model to describe the content and evolution of our Universe \citep{Planck18_I,PlanckCosmo .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Future experiments are now focusing on the fainter polarisation signal of the CMB in order to unveil the earliest phases of our cosmic history.
elements.sent-2411-014: In particular, the standard cosmological model may require a phase of accelerated expansion -- cosmic inflation -- occurring in the very first fraction of a second after the primordial singularity, in order to explain multiple observational facts about the early Universe \citep{inflationhist1,inflationhist2,inflationhist3 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: If this event occurred, it generated a gravitational wave background bathing the primordial plasma, leading to a characteristic large scale polarisation pattern -- the primordial $B$ modes -- imprinted in the CMB \citep{InflationmodesB3,InflationsmodesB1,InflationsmodesB2 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In the current leading hypothesis, the energy density at the origin of cosmic inflation has to be generated by one or multiple novel fundamental fields beyond those of the standard model of particle physics.
elements.sent-2411-017: The detection of the primordial $B$ modes thus represents one of the most ambitious target of modern cosmology for the next decades, allowing to test fundamental physics at energy scales beyond the reach of particle accelerators.
elements.sent-2411-018: The amplitude of the primordial $B$ modes is quantified by the tensor-to-scalar ratio $r$, directly related to the energy scale at which inflation occurred.
elements.sent-2411-019: It is constrained by current data to satisfy $r\lesssim 0.03$ (95\% C.L.) \citep{tristram,Galloni2023 , while contemporary and future missions will probe the value of this parameter at the $10^{-3}$ level in the next decades \citep{SimonsObservatory,Ptep,CMBS4 .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In order to reach such a goal, multiple challenges have to be faced.
elements.sent-2411-021: In particular, our own Galaxy produces complex polarised signals -- known as foregrounds -- which contaminate the CMB and are several orders of magnitude brighter than it.
elements.sent-2411-022: Distinguishing the Galactic from the cosmological signal requires a delicate procedure known as component separation \cite{Dickinson2009,PlanckCompoSep .}
elements.sent-2411-023: As CMB experiments become more and more sensitive to the primordial signal, they accordingly become more and more sensitive to the complexity of the foregrounds \citep{Remazeilles_etal_2016 , thus asking for refined component separation methods.}
elements.sent-2411-024: In the frequency range relevant for CMB observations, the polarised Galactic foreground signal is produced by two main sources: synchrotron emission and thermal dust signal.
elements.sent-2411-025: Synchrotron, dominating at low frequencies, is generated by light charged particles (cosmic rays) accelerating and spiralling around the lines of the Galactic magnetic field (GMF).
elements.sent-2411-026: Thermal dust, dominating at high frequencies, is the consequence of stellar light being absorbed and re-emitted by dust grains populating the ISM and rotating on themselves perpendicularly to the GMF.
elements.sent-2411-027: Local physical conditions in the ISM as temperature, composition, density and orientation of the GMF are varying across the three dimensions of our Galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-028: This fact is supported by both observational and theoretical considerations ranging from the scale of the filaments to the scale of the whole Galaxy \citep{Ferriere, dustacrossMW, Ysard2013, Jaffe2013, vardustdisk2, Planck18_XI,Zelko2022,QUIJOTE2023,Pelgrims2024,Liu2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: The variation of these physical conditions is directly traced by the light charged particles and the dust grains present in the interstellar medium (ISM) and is associated with a 3D variation of their emission properties.
elements.sent-2411-030: The spectral parameters and local polarisation orientation associated to the synchrotron and the thermal dust signals are thus changing over the sky.
elements.sent-2411-031: As such, any astrophysical observation present itself as a mixture of different local signals associated with different emission conditions.
elements.sent-2411-032: Here, we refer to this phenomenon as "mixing" \citep{Chluba2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Mixing occurs along a single line of sight in the 3D Galaxy, in-between line of sights in the plane of the sky (e.g. in the instrumental beam or map pixel) and over large patches of the sky when doing a spherical harmonic transformation.
elements.sent-2411-034: Due to the non-linearity and geometric nature of the local polarised foreground emission, the mixing has significant and interdependent consequences on the total observed signal.
elements.sent-2411-035: First, as the sum of multiple non-linear functions gives a different function, the total (polarised) intensity will not behave as the local spectral energy distribution (SED) anymore.
elements.sent-2411-036: This phenomenon is known as SED distortions \citep{Chluba2017,Remazeilles2018,Mangilli .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Then, as the two Stokes parameters $Q$ and $U$ might be distorted differently, the total polarisation angle will rotate with frequency \citep{tassis,Vacher2022b .}
elements.sent-2411-038: Furthermore, mixing also has consequences for the decomposition of the foreground signal into $E$ and $B$ modes.
elements.sent-2411-039: To the spectral rotation of the polarisation angle corresponds a rotation of the $E$ modes into the $B$ modes, leading to a variation of the $E/B$ ratio of the total signal with frequency \citep{Vacher2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-040: Finally, because of the dependence of the emission properties with the direction of observation, it is not possible to infer a map at a given frequency from the one at another frequency using a constant scaling.
elements.sent-2411-041: Consequently, the cross-frequency angular power spectra will loose some power.
elements.sent-2411-042: This last phenomenon is referred to as "frequency decorrelation" \citep{Planck2016_XXX,pelgrims2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Modelling and measuring all these effects is a priority both for CMB component separation and Galactic science.
elements.sent-2411-044: Indeed, a mismodelling of these properties could have dramatic consequences in the recovering of the primordial $B$ modes through component separation in quest for primordial inflation \citep{PlanckandBICEP or a primordial $EB$ correlation, signature of parity violating cosmic birefringence in the early Universe\footnote{For a discussion on the $EB$ correlation produced by foregrounds and their impact on CMB analysis see also \cite{Clark2021,Cukierman2022,Diego-Palazuelos2022a,Jost2023,Hervias-Caimapo2024 .. \citep{Diego-Palazuelos2022b .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: On the other hand, detecting and understanding the consequences of mixing allows to probe the variation of the properties of the ISM in the 3D Galaxy \citep{pelgrims2021,Ritacco22,McBride2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Moment expansion \citep{Chluba2017 models the SED distortions arising from mixing in a minimal and well motivated way, through a Taylor expansion of the SED with respect to the spectral parameters, a framework that was also applied to the modelling of Sunyaev-Zeldovich signals to separate and compress spectral and spatial information \citep{Chluba2012SZpack .}}
elements.sent-2411-047: It can be generalized to the polarised signal in order to also model the spectral variation of the polarisation angle \citep{Vacher2022b .}
elements.sent-2411-048: The formalism was further extended at the angular power spectra level in intensity and polarisation \citep{Mangilli,Vacher2023 , such that the consequences of mixing can be captured even in harmonic space.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The moments thus represent a very powerful path in order to perform component separation of the CMB foregrounds, using minimum variance approaches \citep{RemazeillesmomentsILC,Rotti2021, Adak2021,Carones2024 or parametric fitting in pixel or harmonic space \citep{Ichiki2019,Azzoni2020,Vacher2022a,Sponseller2022,Minami2023,Azzoni2023,Wolz2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-050: In this work, we use moments for the first time to understand and build thermal dust simulations containing additional complexity arising in the third dimension, from the line of sight variation of the physical conditions.
elements.sent-2411-051: While this exercise can easily be done both in intensity and in polarisation, we will focus on polarisation, aiming our discussion on the impact of such complexity on the measurement of the primordial $B$ modes.
elements.sent-2411-052: The new models will be generated as variations of the ones already provided by the PySM framework, which provides full sky simulations of the foreground signals for the CMB community \citep{Thorne,Zonca2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: Our objective is twofold: firstly, we demonstrate that the moment expansion can be used to decompose and interpret the PySM models already existing and used by the CMB community.
elements.sent-2411-054: Secondly, we show that the moments provide a simple and powerful tool to inject any desired additional complexity to these models.
elements.sent-2411-055: This last point is of particular interest in order to study the limits of various component separation methods.
elements.sent-2411-056: In Sec.~\ref{sec:mom-rev , we give a general presentation of the moment expansion formalism and its ability to model and tackle foreground complexity.}
elements.sent-2411-057: In Sec.~\ref{sec:d12 , we assess quantitatively such ability by reproducing the line of sight complexity of a dust model used by the CMB community using moment expansion.}
elements.sent-2411-058: In Sec.~\ref{sec:model-building , we re-use the moments computed in the previous section to create maximally complex dust models in the limit set by \planck{ data.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: We then apply basic component separation techniques to these models in order to quantify the impact of the added complexity.
elements.sent-2411-060: We further attempt to interpret this impact in regard of the moment maps contained in the models.
elements.sent-2411-061: Finally, we discuss the limits of our analysis and present our conclusions in Sec.~\ref{sec:conclusion .}

elements.sent-2411-075: Considering any microwave sky observation, mixing can occur in three different ways: i) along the line of sight in the depth of the sky, ii) in between lines of sights inside the instrumental beam and/or the sky pixel or iii) in between lines of sights over large patches of the sky when performing a spherical harmonic transformation.
elements.sent-2411-076: The consequences of ii) and iii) will depend on the instrumental setup as well as the framework used to analyse the data\footnote{In particular, pixel based component separation methods will not suffer from iii) but will be majorly impacted by ii).}
elements.sent-2411-077: This impact will not only be caused by the convolution of the instrumental beam but also by some required under-pixelization of the data (downgrading) as well as how the beam is treated (on this last point see \cite{Rizzieri2024 ).}
elements.sent-2411-078: It is common to distinguish the consequences of i) and ii) by naming them respectively "line-of-sight" and "plane of sky" complexities.
elements.sent-2411-079: However, in practice, it is impossible to create an absolute distinction between these two effects in general and they can all be modelled using the same formalism as both i) and ii) will appear identically in Eq.~\eqref{eq:mixing .}
elements.sent-2411-080: In this work, we will attempt to avoid this confusion by remaining in the context of building a model on a pixelized sphere and by distinguishing "intra-pixel" and "inter-pixel" complexities to describe respectively i) and ii) at a fixed pixel resolution.

elements.subsection-2411-003: Modelling the signal in each pixel
elements.sent-2411-104: We will now illustrate the power of the moment formalism both to model and understand the 3D complexity contained within a sky map.
elements.sent-2411-105: To do so, we will use the foreground models available within the Python Sky Model (PySM) package\footnote{\url{https://github.com/galsci/pysm \citep{Thorne,Zonca2021 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-106: PySM is a python software providing different types of data-driven full-sky simulations for the Galactic emissions.
elements.sent-2411-107: These simulations are largely used by the CMB community to apply and test component separation methods.

elements.sent-2411-301: Still, we conclude that special care has to be given on attempts at assessing the complexity of a foreground model since such a question is deeply dependent on the component separation method under consideration.
elements.sent-2411-302: Different methods are sensitive to different facets of foreground complexity, and the moment coefficients provide a powerful tool to separate and study these different aspects.
elements.sent-2411-303: Still, we were able to propose novel alternative models -- such as model B -- which present a significant challenge for both parametric and minimum variance methods while remaining compatible with data.
elements.sent-2411-304: In this work, we demonstrated that the use of moments maps provide a powerful tool to quantify and model the complexity of the foreground SED models in a minimal and simple way.
elements.sent-2411-305: In principle, the complexity of virtually any foreground model currently used by the CMB community can be re-expressed by a set of moment maps.
elements.sent-2411-306: This complexity can then be amplified or reduced in a consistent way by changing the amplitude or patterns of these maps as desired.
elements.sent-2411-307: Doing so allows to perform careful studies of the link between the complexity contained in the maps and the impact of this complexity on the power spectra and component separation.
elements.sent-2411-308: In our analysis, we provided a first application of this concept by reproducing accurately the most complex PySM dust model (\dtwelve{ ) in terms of moment maps.}
elements.sent-2411-309: We were able to reproduce such a model at the one per ten thousand level using five moment maps at second order of the expansion.
elements.sent-2411-310: Doing so, we learned that despite their small amplitudes, the imaginary parts of the first order moments are the main drivers of $B$-modes decorrelation.
elements.sent-2411-311: The expansion must however be pushed up to second order in order to recover accurately the dependence of the $E/B$ ratio with frequency.
elements.sent-2411-312: In a second step, we re-used \dtwelve{ 's moment maps to inject some additional complexity in each pixel of a simpler foreground model ($\dten{}$), simulating the presence of mixing along the line of sight.}
elements.sent-2411-313: This injection was done in a self-consistent fashion, which can be interpreted in term of interstellar medium physics.
elements.sent-2411-314: By scaling these moment maps, we were able to build extremely complex models with regard to the proxies of complexity given by decorrelation of the $B$-modes $\Delta_\ell^{BB}$ and spectral dependence of the $E$-to-$B$ ratio $r^{E/B _\nu$.}
elements.sent-2411-315: As such, we proved that all the models currently used by the CMB community (including \dtwelve{ ) contain much milder distortions coming from the third dimension than what can be allowed by data.}
elements.sent-2411-316: It is thus crucial to assess whether component separation methods are robust enough to handle these novels complex scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-317: Using component separation on the newly constructed models, we saw that the additional complexity could have a significant -- but not insurmountable -- impact on the quality of the reconstruction of CMB polarisation fields.
elements.sent-2411-318: Quite interestingly, different component separation methods are not sensitive identically to the same kind of complexity, such that they do not agree on what is the most complex model.
elements.sent-2411-319: Parametric methods, as FGBuster, are very sensitive to deviations of the signal from a canonical SED.
elements.sent-2411-320: They are thus more impacted by models having significant decorrelation and frequency-dependence of the $E/B$ ratio, which can be largely driven by the imaginary part of the first order moment maps, as discussed in Sec.~\ref{sec:d12 .}
elements.sent-2411-321: On the other hand, minimum variance methods are pretty much insensitive to this complexity, but are more sensitive to the total power added by the moment maps to the foreground signal.
elements.sent-2411-322: They are thus sensitive to the modulus of the moment maps, regardless of whether the complexity is coming from their real or imaginary part.
elements.sent-2411-323: Thus, a lesson learned from this investigation is that caution is required when assessing the "complexity" of a model as the different quantifiers of this complexity can depict contradictory stories.
elements.sent-2411-324: The models studied here must be understood as toy models allowing to investigate the relevance of the moment expansion formalism for foreground modelling as well as the impact of some possible additional complexity in the third dimension.
elements.sent-2411-325: However, it is clear that none of these models can pretend to be physical and representative of the actual or expected complexity of the sky.
elements.sent-2411-326: Clearly, the moment maps used in this work do not contain the non-Gaussian complex structure characteristic of the cosmic dust signal.
elements.sent-2411-327: While it has been recently suggested by \cite{Abril-Cabezas2024 that component separation for the CMB is largely insensitive to such non-gaussianities, the impact of non-Gaussian moments has yet never been discussed.}
elements.sent-2411-328: Powerful tools, as Minkowski functionals \citep{Carones2022,2024JCAP...01..039C and more generically scattering transforms \citep{Allys2019,Delouis2022,Mousset2024 could be used to study and model these non-Gaussianities on the sphere.}}
elements.sent-2411-329: On the long term, measurements as the ones of \cite{Zelko2022 and \cite{Pelgrims2024 should be used in order to put refined bounds and build more realistic and reliable moment maps representing the actual complexity contained in the third dimension.}}
elements.sent-2411-330: Until then, one remains free to tweak as desired the morphologies and amplitudes of the added moment maps in order to introduce and study the effect of non-Gaussianities or any other kind of complexity.
elements.sent-2411-331: The present analysis focused on the impact of thermal dust on the recovery of the primordial $B$ modes.
elements.sent-2411-332: However, it would be straightforward and beneficial to apply again such a methodology in other contexts.
elements.sent-2411-333: Firstly, the complexity of any type of foreground signal other than dust can be similarly interpreted, decomposed and amplified using moment maps.
elements.sent-2411-334: A natural step would then be to repeat the present work focusing on the synchrotron emission.
elements.sent-2411-335: Secondly, additional line of sight complexity must also impact at some level the recovery of the CMB $EB$ spectra.
elements.sent-2411-336: To assess this impact, one must consider the addition of the moment spectral complexity to a model containing a significant $EB$ foreground signal, as the one proposed in \cite{Hervias-Caimapo2024 and see whether or not analysis as the ones proposed in \cite{Diego-Palazuelos2022b,Jost2023 remain robust in such maximally complex scenarios.}}
elements.sent-2411-337: As such, more general studies providing a systematic link between different sets of moment maps and the resulting 3D complexity for various types of foregrounds and analysis are much desired and will be provided in future works.

elements.sent-2411-416: In the present work, we demonstrate that the spin-moment expansion formalism provides a powerful framework to model and understand this complexity, and we put special focus on the effects that arise from variations of the physical conditions along each line of sight on the sky.
elements.sent-2411-417: We performed the first application of the moment expansion to reproduce a thermal dust model largely used by the CMB community, demonstrating its power as a minimal tool to compress, understand, and model the information contained within any foreground model.
elements.sent-2411-418: Furthermore, we used this framework to produce new models of thermal dust emission containing the maximal amount of complexity allowed by the current data while remaining compatible with the observed angular power spectra by the \planck{ mission.}
elements.sent-2411-419: Our modern understanding of cosmology relies heavily on the precise characterisation of the cosmic microwave background (CMB) signal.
elements.sent-2411-420: In particular, the standard cosmological model may require a phase of accelerated expansion -- cosmic inflation -- occurring in the very first fraction of a second after the primordial singularity to explain multiple observational facts about the early Universe \citep{inflationhist1,inflationhist2,inflationhist3 .}
elements.sent-2411-421: If this event occurred, it generated a gravitational wave background bathing the primordial plasma and led to a characteristic large-scale polarisation pattern -- the primordial $B$ modes -- being imprinted in the CMB \citep{InflationmodesB3,InflationsmodesB1,InflationsmodesB2 .}
elements.sent-2411-422: Detection of the primordial $B$ modes thus represents one of the most ambitious targets of modern cosmology for the next decades, but it will allow for testing of fundamental physics at energy scales beyond the reach of particle accelerators.
elements.sent-2411-423: The amplitude of the primordial $B$ modes is quantified by the tensor-to-scalar ratio $r$, which is directly related to the energy scale at which inflation occurred.
elements.sent-2411-424: It is constrained by current data to satisfy $r\lesssim 0.03$ (95\% C.L.) \citep{tristram,Galloni2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-425: However, contemporary and future missions will probe the value of this parameter at the $10^{-3}$ level in the next decades \citep{SimonsObservatory,Ptep,CMBS4 .}
elements.sent-2411-426: In particular, our own Galaxy produces complex polarised signals -- known as foregrounds -- that contaminate the CMB and are several orders of magnitude brighter than it.
elements.sent-2411-427: Distinguishing the Galactic signal from the cosmological signal requires a delicate procedure known as component separation \citep{Dickinson2009,PlanckCompoSep .}
elements.sent-2411-428: As CMB experiments become increasingly sensitive to the primordial signal, they accordingly become more sensitive to the complexity of the foregrounds \citep{Remazeilles_etal_2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-429: Thus, they require refined component separation methods.
elements.sent-2411-430: Thermal dust, dominating at high frequencies, is the consequence of stellar light being absorbed and re-emitted by dust grains populating the interstellar medium (ISM) and rotating on themselves perpendicularly to the GMF.
elements.sent-2411-431: Local physical conditions in the ISM such as temperature, composition, density, and orientation of the GMF vary across the three dimensions of the Galaxy.
elements.sent-2411-432: The variation of these physical conditions is directly traced by the light charged particles and the dust grains present in the ISM and is associated with a 3D variation of their emission properties.
elements.sent-2411-433: The spectral parameters and local polarisation orientation associated to the synchrotron and the thermal dust signals thus change over the sky.
elements.sent-2411-434: As such, any astrophysical observation presents itself as a mixture of different local signals associated with different emission conditions.
elements.sent-2411-435: Here, we refer to this phenomenon as `mixing' \citep{Chluba2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-436: Mixing occurs along a single line of sight in the 3D Galaxy, between lines of sight in the plane of the sky (e.g. in the instrumental beam or map pixel), and over large patches of the sky when a spherical harmonic transformation is performed.
elements.sent-2411-437: This phenomenon is known as `SED distortions\ \citep{Chluba2017,Remazeilles2018,Mangilli .}
elements.sent-2411-438: Finally, because of the dependence of the emission properties on the direction of an observation, it is not possible to infer a map at a given frequency based on one at another frequency using a constant scaling.
elements.sent-2411-439: Consequently, the cross-frequency angular power spectra loose some power.
elements.sent-2411-440: This last phenomenon is referred to as `frequency decorrelation' \citep{Planck2016_XXX,pelgrims2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-441: Modelling and measuring all of these effects is a priority for both CMB component separation and Galactic science.
elements.sent-2411-442: Indeed, mismodelling of these properties could have dramatic consequences on the recovery of the primordial $B$ modes through component separation in the quest for primordial inflation \citep{PlanckandBICEP or in the quest for a primordial $EB$ correlation , signature of parity violating cosmic birefringence in the early Universe\footnote{For a discussion on the $EB$ correlation produced by foregrounds and their impact on CMB analysis, see also \cite{Clark2021,Cukierman2022,Diego-Palazuelos2022a,Jost2023,Hervias-Caimapo2024 .. \citep{Diego-Palazuelos2022b .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-443: On the other hand, detecting and understanding the consequences of mixing allows one to probe the variation of the properties of the ISM in the 3D Galaxy \citep{pelgrims2021,Ritacco22,McBride2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-444: Moment expansion \citep{Chluba2017 models the SED distortions arising from mixing in a minimal and well motivated way through a Taylor expansion of the SED with respect to the spectral parameters, which is a framework that was also applied to the modelling of Sunyaev-Zeldovich signals to separate and compress spectral and spatial information \citep{Chluba2012SZpack .}}
elements.sent-2411-445: This approach can be generalisedd to the polarised signal in order to also model the spectral variation of the polarisation angle \citep{Vacher2022b .}
elements.sent-2411-446: The formalism was further extended at the angular power spectra level in intensity and polarisation \citep{Mangilli,Vacher2023 such that the consequences of mixing can be captured even in harmonic space.}
elements.sent-2411-447: The moments thus represent a very powerful path towards performing component separation of the CMB foregrounds using minimum variance approaches \citep{RemazeillesmomentsILC,Rotti2021, Adak2021,Carones2024 or parametric fitting in pixel or harmonic space \citep{Ichiki2019,Azzoni2020,Vacher2022a,Sponseller2022,Minami2023,Azzoni2023,Wolz2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-448: In this work, we use moments for the first time to understand and build thermal dust simulations containing additional complexity arising in the third dimension from the line of sight variation of the physical conditions.
elements.sent-2411-449: While this exercise can easily be done both in intensity and in polarisation, we focus on polarisation, aiming our discussion on the impact of such complexity on the measurement of the primordial $B$ modes.
elements.sent-2411-451: First, we seek to demonstrate that the moment expansion can be used to decompose and interpret the PySM models already existing and used by the CMB community.
elements.sent-2411-452: Secondly, we aim to show that the moments provide a simple and powerful tool to inject any desired additional complexity into these models.
elements.sent-2411-453: This last point is of particular interest to the study of the limits of various component separation methods.

elements.sent-2411-547: More precisely, the relative power is driven by the modulus of the moment maps, whose main contribution comes from their real parts, which are significantly larger in amplitude than their imaginary parts (see Appendix ~\ref{app:mom-maps ).}
elements.sent-2411-548: Therefore, model B, for which the moments are rigidly amplified by a constant factor, turns out to have the largest moment modulus and thus the largest relative power, and it turns out to be the most complex for NILC.
elements.sent-2411-549: On the other hand, minimum variance approaches do not assume a model, and therefore, their performance does not depend on the origin of the complexity, that is, whether the complexity arises from 2D (inter-pixel) or 3D (intra-pixel) distortions.
elements.sent-2411-550: A more realistic analysis would also have to deal with the complex coupling between systematic effects (calibration of the polarisation angle, far side lobe leakages, gain uncertainties ...) and the additional complexity added to the foreground.
elements.sent-2411-551: In many cases, the foreground and the systematic complexity become entangled, such that it becomes an extremely difficult task to separate them.
elements.sent-2411-552: In this work, we have demonstrated that the use of moment maps provides a powerful tool to quantify and model the complexity of the foreground SED models in a minimal and simple way.
elements.sent-2411-553: This approach allows one to perform careful studies of the link between the complexity contained in the maps and the impact of this complexity on the power spectra and component separation.
elements.sent-2411-554: In our analysis, we provided a first application of this concept by accurately reproducing the most complex PySM dust model (\dtwelve{ ) in terms of moment maps.}
elements.sent-2411-555: We were able to reproduce such a model at the one-per-ten-thousand level using five moment maps at the second order of the expansion.
elements.sent-2411-556: In doing so, we learned that despite their small amplitudes, the imaginary parts of the first order moments are the main drivers of $B$-mode decorrelation.
elements.sent-2411-557: The expansion, however, must be pushed up to the second order to accurately recover the dependence of the $E/B$ ratio with frequency.
elements.sent-2411-558: In a second step, we re-used \dtwelve{ 's moment maps to inject some additional complexity into each pixel of a simpler foreground model ($\dten{}$), simulating the presence of mixing along the line of sight.}
elements.sent-2411-559: This injection was done in a self-consistent fashion, which can be interpreted in terms of ISM physics.
elements.sent-2411-560: It is thus crucial to assess whether component separation methods are robust enough to handle these new complex scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-561: Quite interestingly, different component separation methods are not sensitive to the same kind of complexity in the same way to the point that they do not agree on what is the most complex model.
elements.sent-2411-562: Parametric methods, such as FGBuster, are very sensitive to deviations of the signal from a canonical SED.
elements.sent-2411-563: They are thus more impacted by models having significant decorrelation and frequency dependence of the $E/B$ ratio, which can be largely driven by the imaginary part of the first order moment maps, as discussed in Sect.~\ref{sec:d12 .}
elements.sent-2411-564: On the other hand, minimum variance methods are pretty much insensitive to this complexity but are more sensitive to the total power added by the moment maps to the foreground signal.
elements.sent-2411-565: They are thus sensitive to the modulus of the moment maps regardless of whether the complexity is coming from their real or imaginary part.
elements.sent-2411-566: A lesson therefore learned from this investigation is that caution is required when assessing the `complexity' of a model, as the different quantifiers of this complexity can depict contradictory stories.
elements.sent-2411-567: The models studied here must be understood as toy models that allow for the investigation of the relevance of the moment expansion formalism for foreground modelling as well as the impact of some possible additional complexity in the third dimension.
elements.sent-2411-568: While it has been recently suggested by \cite{Abril-Cabezas2024 that component separation for the CMB is largely insensitive to such non-Gaussianities, the impact of non-Gaussian moments has never been discussed as of yet.}

elements.sent-2411-570: Doing so ensured that the sum of the inter-pixel complexity and the added intra-pixel complexity is statistically compatible with current data.
